source.title: Thyroid Nodules: Advances in Evaluation and Management

signals: primary goal of thyroid nodule evaluation is to determine whether it is malignant
fine-needle aspiration based on ultrasound characteristics and size
Bethesda System (categories 1 through 6) is used to classify samples
workup for thyroid nodules, including how to interpret ultrasound findings

extraction.summary: Thyroid nodules are common, found in up to 68% of the population by ultrasound, but 90-95% are benign. The primary goal of evaluation is to determine malignancy risk through TSH measurement, ultrasound assessment, and selective fine-needle aspiration (FNA) based on size and suspicious features.

population.age_criteria: More common with increasing age; micropapillary cancers in patients <40 years may have more progressive disease
population.risk_criteria: Four times more common in women; risk factors for malignancy include ionizing radiation exposure (especially when young), rapid nodule growth, hoarseness, family history of thyroid cancer or cancer syndromes (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2, familial adenomatous polyposis), Graves disease patients with hypofunctioning nodules
population.disease_definition: Thyroid nodules detected by palpation or imaging, classified as functioning ('hot') or nonfunctioning ('cold') based on radionuclide uptake
population.exclusions: Pure cystic nodules rarely malignant; hyperfunctioning nodules rarely malignant
population.severity_staging: Bethesda System categories 1-6: (1) Nondiagnostic 1-4% malignancy risk, (2) Benign 0-3%, (3) Atypia/follicular lesion 5-15%, (4) Follicular neoplasm 15-30%, (5) Suspicious 60-75%, (6) Malignant 97-99%

key_thresholds.context: Cancers <1 cm may be followed with observation
May have higher malignancy prevalence and reduced FNA accuracy

key_thresholds.parameter: Nodule size for FNA in children
key_thresholds.value: Ultrasound features rather than size alone
key_thresholds.context: Pediatric evaluation - thyroid volume increases with age
Nodules ≥4 cm evaluation for cancer exclusion

recommendations.recommendation: Perform thyroid ultrasonography with cervical lymph node survey in all patients with thyroid nodules
Measure serum TSH during initial evaluation; if low, perform radionuclide thyroid uptake scan
recommendations.notes: Determines if nodule is functioning or nonfunctioning

recommendations.recommendation: FNA recommended for thyroid nodules ≥1 cm with suspicious ultrasound pattern
recommendations.notes: Suspicious features: hypoechoic echogenicity, solid composition, irregular margins, microcalcifications, height > width, extrathyroidal extension, disrupted rim calcification, cervical lymphadenopathy

recommendations.recommendation: Do not perform FNA on nodules <1 cm except for highly suspicious features or patient <40 years
recommendations.notes: Exception: extrathyroidal extension or cervical lymphadenopathy

recommendations.recommendation: Counsel patients about benefits and limitations before molecular testing
recommendations.notes: Obtain informed consent before ordering molecular testing

recommendations.recommendation: Use molecular testing to risk-stratify indeterminate thyroid nodules (Bethesda 3-4)
recommendations.notes: Can guide surgical vs conservative management

recommendations.recommendation: Do not use nuclear medicine thyroid scans in patients with normal thyroid function
Use ultrasound features rather than size alone to identify nodules requiring FNA in children
recommendations.notes: Due to age-related thyroid volume changes

recommendations.recommendation: Surgery recommended for children with indeterminate FNA results
recommendations.notes: Molecular testing not validated in children

recommendations.recommendation: Molecular testing can be used for indeterminate thyroid nodules in adults
recommendations.notes: Multiple genomic classifiers available including ThyroSeq v3, miRNA-based assays

recommendations.recommendation: Follow Bethesda System for reporting thyroid cytopathology

medications.indication: Radionuclide thyroid uptake scan when TSH is low
Treatment of hyperthyroidism when radioiodine contraindicated or not preferred
Not recommended for benign nodules due to harm/benefit ratio

red_flags: Hoarseness suggesting recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement
Family history of thyroid cancer or cancer syndromes
History of ionizing radiation exposure especially at young age
Bethesda category 5 (suspicious for malignancy) or 6 (malignant) cytology
extraction.follow_up: Benign nodules: repeat ultrasonography at 12-24 months, then extend to 3-5 years if stable. Indeterminate nodules with benign molecular testing: ultrasonography at 12-18 months. Nondiagnostic FNA: repeat in 6-12 weeks. Nodules <1 cm with suspicious features: ultrasonography in 6-9 months.
extraction.escalation_path: Refer for surgery: Bethesda 5-6 cytology, indeterminate cytology with suspicious molecular testing, hyperfunctioning nodules requiring definitive treatment. Endocrinology consultation: uncertainty in molecular test interpretation, active surveillance consideration for micropapillary cancer, complex cases. Repeat FNA: significant nodule growth, nondiagnostic initial sample, Bethesda 3 without molecular testing.
